clusters.title: Most Americans don’t know this food raises colon cancer risk
clusters.summary: Nearly half of Americans don’t know that processed meat increases colorectal cancer risk, according to a new poll. But once they learn the connection, most support warning labels—suggesting people want clearer information. Experts warn that awareness is lagging even among healthcare providers.

items.title: Most Americans don’t know this food raises colon cancer risk
items.description: Nearly half of Americans don’t know that processed meat increases colorectal cancer risk, according to a new poll. But once they learn the connection, most support warning labels—suggesting people want clearer information. Experts warn that awareness is lagging even among healthcare providers. The good news: diets rich in plant foods and fiber, along with healthy habits, can dramatically lower risk.

clusters.title: Scientists discover brain switches that clear Alzheimer’s plaques
clusters.summary: Researchers have identified two brain receptors that help the brain clear away amyloid beta, a hallmark of Alzheimer’s disease. By stimulating these receptors in mice, scientists increased levels of a natural amyloid-breaking enzyme, reduced buildup in the brain, and improved memory-related behavior. Because these receptors are common drug targets, the findings could open the door to affordable pill-based treatments with fewer side effects.

items.title: Scientists discover brain switches that clear Alzheimer’s plaques
items.description: Researchers have identified two brain receptors that help the brain clear away amyloid beta, a hallmark of Alzheimer’s disease. By stimulating these receptors in mice, scientists increased levels of a natural amyloid-breaking enzyme, reduced buildup in the brain, and improved memory-related behavior. Because these receptors are common drug targets, the findings could open the door to affordable pill-based treatments with fewer side effects.

clusters.title: First ever atomic movie reveals hidden driver of radiation damage
clusters.summary: Researchers have visualized atoms in motion just before a radiation-driven decay process occurs, revealing a surprisingly dynamic scene. Instead of remaining fixed, the atoms roam and rearrange, directly influencing how and when the decay unfolds. This “atomic movie” shows that structure and motion play a central role in radiation damage mechanisms.

items.title: First ever atomic movie reveals hidden driver of radiation damage
items.description: Researchers have visualized atoms in motion just before a radiation-driven decay process occurs, revealing a surprisingly dynamic scene. Instead of remaining fixed, the atoms roam and rearrange, directly influencing how and when the decay unfolds. This “atomic movie” shows that structure and motion play a central role in radiation damage mechanisms. The findings could improve our understanding of how harmful radiation affects biological matter.

clusters.title: Playing Call of Duty before bed doesn’t ruin sleep, and it might even boost your memory
clusters.summary: A recent experiment reveals that playing an action video game for an hour before bed can reduce stress and improve visuospatial memory the next day without disrupting objective sleep quality.

items.title: Playing Call of Duty before bed doesn’t ruin sleep, and it might even boost your memory
items.description: A recent experiment reveals that playing an action video game for an hour before bed can reduce stress and improve visuospatial memory the next day without disrupting objective sleep quality.

clusters.title: Scientists discover surprising brain trigger behind high blood pressure
clusters.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising brain-based trigger for high blood pressure, tracing it to a small region in the brainstem that normally controls breathing. This area, which kicks in during forceful exhalations like coughing, laughing, or exercise, also appears to activate nerves that tighten blood vessels—raising blood pressure. When researchers switched off this region in experiments, blood pressure dropped back to normal, suggesting it plays a direct role in hypertension.

items.title: Scientists discover surprising brain trigger behind high blood pressure
items.description: Scientists have uncovered a surprising brain-based trigger for high blood pressure, tracing it to a small region in the brainstem that normally controls breathing. This area, which kicks in during forceful exhalations like coughing, laughing, or exercise, also appears to activate nerves that tighten blood vessels—raising blood pressure. When researchers switched off this region in experiments, blood pressure dropped back to normal, suggesting it plays a direct role in hypertension.

clusters.title: Scientists find immune cell linked to long COVID fatigue and symptoms
clusters.summary: Long COVID remains a frustrating medical mystery, affecting up to 1 in 10 people long after the initial infection fades. Now, scientists have uncovered a crucial clue hidden deep within the immune system. By analyzing individual immune cells, they identified a distinct molecular state in key white blood cells—especially common in patients who initially had mild or moderate COVID.

items.title: Scientists find immune cell linked to long COVID fatigue and symptoms
items.description: Long COVID remains a frustrating medical mystery, affecting up to 1 in 10 people long after the initial infection fades. Now, scientists have uncovered a crucial clue hidden deep within the immune system. By analyzing individual immune cells, they identified a distinct molecular state in key white blood cells—especially common in patients who initially had mild or moderate COVID.

clusters.title: Boosting a key brain protein could help treat Rett syndrome
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a new way to increase a key brain protein damaged in Rett syndrome, a rare genetic disorder that affects thousands of children worldwide. Early studies in mice and patient-derived cells show the approach can restore normal brain cell function, raising hopes for future therapies.

items.title: Boosting a key brain protein could help treat Rett syndrome
items.description: Researchers have discovered a new way to increase a key brain protein damaged in Rett syndrome, a rare genetic disorder that affects thousands of children worldwide. Early studies in mice and patient-derived cells show the approach can restore normal brain cell function, raising hopes for future therapies.

clusters.title: Weight loss drug Ozempic cuts depression, anxiety, and addiction risk
clusters.summary: GLP-1 medications like semaglutide (Ozempic) may offer unexpected mental health benefits alongside weight loss. A large study found major drops in depression, anxiety, and psychiatric-related hospital visits among users. Even substance use disorders were significantly lower during treatment.

items.title: Weight loss drug Ozempic cuts depression, anxiety, and addiction risk
items.description: GLP-1 medications like semaglutide (Ozempic) may offer unexpected mental health benefits alongside weight loss. A large study found major drops in depression, anxiety, and psychiatric-related hospital visits among users. Even substance use disorders were significantly lower during treatment. Researchers suspect both lifestyle improvements and direct brain effects could be at play.

clusters.title: Cutting sweet foods doesn’t reduce cravings or improve health
clusters.summary: A new study suggests that eating less sweet food doesn’t make people crave it any less—or improve their health markers. Participants who increased or decreased sweetness in their diets showed no changes in preferences, weight, or disease risk. Many even returned to their old eating habits over time.

items.title: Cutting sweet foods doesn’t reduce cravings or improve health
items.description: A new study suggests that eating less sweet food doesn’t make people crave it any less—or improve their health markers. Participants who increased or decreased sweetness in their diets showed no changes in preferences, weight, or disease risk. Many even returned to their old eating habits over time. Researchers say it may be time to rethink guidelines that focus on cutting sweetness instead of reducing sugar and calories.

clusters.title: Ultra-processed foods linked to 67% higher risk of heart attack and stroke
clusters.summary: Eating large amounts of ultra-processed foods like chips, frozen meals, sugary drinks, and packaged snacks may significantly raise the risk of serious heart problems. In a major U.S. study, people consuming around nine servings per day had a 67% higher risk of heart attacks, strokes, or death from heart disease compared to those eating about one serving.

items.title: Ultra-processed foods linked to 67% higher risk of heart attack and stroke
items.description: Eating large amounts of ultra-processed foods like chips, frozen meals, sugary drinks, and packaged snacks may significantly raise the risk of serious heart problems. In a major U.S. study, people consuming around nine servings per day had a 67% higher risk of heart attacks, strokes, or death from heart disease compared to those eating about one serving. The risk didn’t just jump at high levels either. Each additional daily serving increased the likelihood of these events by more than 5%, even after accounting for calories, overall diet quality, and common health conditions.

clusters.title: You don’t need to lose weight to reverse prediabetes, study finds
clusters.summary: For years, people with prediabetes have been told the same thing: lose weight or risk developing diabetes. But new research flips that idea on its head, showing that blood sugar can return to normal even without shedding pounds. The key isn’t just how much fat you carry—it’s where it’s stored.

items.title: You don’t need to lose weight to reverse prediabetes, study finds
items.description: For years, people with prediabetes have been told the same thing: lose weight or risk developing diabetes. But new research flips that idea on its head, showing that blood sugar can return to normal even without shedding pounds. The key isn’t just how much fat you carry—it’s where it’s stored. Harmful fat deep in the abdomen fuels inflammation and disrupts insulin, while fat under the skin can actually support healthier metabolism.

clusters.title: The mental health gap between teen boys and girls is growing in progressive nations
clusters.summary: A new study analyzing two decades of international data reveals that the mental health gap between teen boys and girls has expanded faster in countries with high gender equality. Rising academic pressure on young women partly explains the divide.

items.title: The mental health gap between teen boys and girls is growing in progressive nations
items.description: A new study analyzing two decades of international data reveals that the mental health gap between teen boys and girls has expanded faster in countries with high gender equality. Rising academic pressure on young women partly explains the divide.

clusters.title: Menstrual hormones may worsen ADHD symptoms in medicated women
clusters.summary: New research shows that ADHD symptoms and negative moods can peak during menstruation. The findings suggest that clinicians and patients may need to account for hormonal fluctuations when planning daily tasks or designing effective prescription treatment regimens.

items.title: Menstrual hormones may worsen ADHD symptoms in medicated women
items.description: New research shows that ADHD symptoms and negative moods can peak during menstruation. The findings suggest that clinicians and patients may need to account for hormonal fluctuations when planning daily tasks or designing effective prescription treatment regimens.

clusters.title: Scientists discover hormone that may stop chronic back pain at its source
clusters.summary: A new study suggests a widely used bone hormone could help relieve chronic back pain in an unexpected way. Instead of just strengthening bone, it appears to stop pain-sensing nerves from growing into damaged spinal areas. In animal models, this led to stronger spinal tissue and reduced pain sensitivity.

items.title: Scientists discover hormone that may stop chronic back pain at its source
items.description: A new study suggests a widely used bone hormone could help relieve chronic back pain in an unexpected way. Instead of just strengthening bone, it appears to stop pain-sensing nerves from growing into damaged spinal areas. In animal models, this led to stronger spinal tissue and reduced pain sensitivity. The findings hint at a future treatment that tackles back pain at its biological roots.

clusters.title: Insulin pills may soon replace daily injections
clusters.summary: For over a century, scientists have chased the dream of insulin pills, but the digestive system kept destroying the drug before it could work—forcing millions of patients to rely on daily injections. Now, researchers at Kumamoto University have developed a clever workaround using a tiny peptide that helps insulin slip through the intestinal wall.

items.title: Insulin pills may soon replace daily injections
items.description: For over a century, scientists have chased the dream of insulin pills, but the digestive system kept destroying the drug before it could work—forcing millions of patients to rely on daily injections. Now, researchers at Kumamoto University have developed a clever workaround using a tiny peptide that helps insulin slip through the intestinal wall.

clusters.title: Brain development may continue into your 30s, new research shows
clusters.summary: That viral claim that your frontal lobe “isn’t fully developed until 25” turns out to be more myth than milestone. Early brain scans showed that gray matter changes dramatically through the teen years, and because studies stopped around age 20, scientists estimated development might wrap up in the mid-20s. But newer, massive brain-imaging research paints a different picture: key wiring and network efficiency in the brain continue evolving into the early 30s.

items.title: Brain development may continue into your 30s, new research shows
items.description: That viral claim that your frontal lobe “isn’t fully developed until 25” turns out to be more myth than milestone. Early brain scans showed that gray matter changes dramatically through the teen years, and because studies stopped around age 20, scientists estimated development might wrap up in the mid-20s. But newer, massive brain-imaging research paints a different picture: key wiring and network efficiency in the brain continue evolving into the early 30s.

clusters.title: Common pesticide may more than double Parkinson’s disease risk
clusters.summary: A new UCLA Health study suggests that long-term exposure to the pesticide chlorpyrifos may dramatically raise the risk of Parkinson’s disease. Researchers found that people living in areas with sustained exposure had more than 2.5 times the likelihood of developing the disorder. Lab experiments reinforced the finding: animals exposed to the chemical developed movement problems, lost dopamine-producing neurons, and showed the same toxic protein buildup seen in Parkinson’s patients.

clusters.title: Will Insurance Cover Ozempic for Sleep Apnea?
clusters.summary: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1s) like Ozempic can help with weight loss, which may assist with sleep apnea symptoms. If your sleep apnea has worsened with weight gain or you have other medical conditions that warrant the use of GLP-1s, your healthcare provider may prescribe one, but they can be pricey. clusters.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation.

items.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
items.description: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation. The finding could help pave the way for earlier diagnosis and more personalized treatments.

clusters.title: Elinzanetant reduces severity and frequency of hot flashes and night sweats in breast cancer patients: Clinical trial
clusters.summary: Results from the OASIS 4 clinical trial show that a new drug, elinzanetant, relieved hot flashes and night sweats that can occur because of the menopause or because of hormone treatment for breast cancer. Now, a new analysis of data from the trial shows that it also gives sustained relief regardless of the type of hormone treatment cancer patients receive.
